chunk_summary: This section discusses macronutrient modifications and feeding methods tailored to chronic pediatric diseases, emphasizing disease-specific mechanisms like energy expenditure, malabsorption, and dysphagia, with numeric guidelines for conditions like CF, Crohn’s disease, and cholestatic liver disease.
learning_objectives: Understand how to tailor macronutrient modifications based on disease-specific mechanisms in pediatric patients.
Identify appropriate feeding methods, including enteral feeding, for children with chronic diseases like CF, Crohn’s disease, and neurodisability.
Learn numeric energy and macronutrient requirements for specific conditions such as CF and cholestatic liver disease.
Recognize the role of nutrition as therapy, such as exclusive enteral nutrition in pediatric Crohn’s disease.
key_facts: In CF, energy needs are 110–200% of standard after age 2, with a high-energy, high-fat diet and pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy.
Exclusive enteral nutrition can induce remission in pediatric Crohn’s disease and must be specialist-led.
Children with chronic cholestatic liver disease may require higher total fat intake with a major MCT component and fat-soluble vitamin replacement.
Neurodisability with dysphagia requires safe energy density strategies, including texture modification and enteral feeding if oral intake is insufficient.
During intensive oncology therapy, early nutrition screening and high-protein/high-calorie intake are critical to counter reduced intake.
reference_quote: In CF, energy needs are often markedly elevated (commonly 110–200% of standard after age 2); a high-energy, higher-fat pattern with specialist-directed pancreatic enzyme replacement is standard.
document_key_topics: Macronutrient modifications in chronic pediatric diseases
Disease-specific feeding methods and enteral nutrition
Energy and nutrient requirements in CF, Crohn’s disease, and cholestatic liver disease
Nutrition as therapy in pediatric chronic conditions
